anchor.snippet: the time complexity is the computational complexity that describes the amount of computer time it takes to run an algorithm
the worst-case time complexity , which is the maximum amount of time required for inputs of a given size
the average-case complexity , which is the average of the time taken on inputs of a given size
is a linear time algorithm and an algorithm with time complexity
An algorithm is termed constant time
accessing a specific element in an array is a constant-time operation, as it requires only a single operation to locate that element
determining the minimum value in an unordered array is not constant time; it requires examining each element
a task that involves swapping the values of a and b to ensure
An algorithm is said to take logarithmic time when
An example of logarithmic time is given by dictionary search.
An algorithm is said to run in polylogarithmic time if its time
matrix chain ordering can be solved in polylogarithmic time on a parallel random-access machine
An algorithm is said to run in sub-linear time (often spelled sublinear time ) if
The specific term sublinear time algorithm commonly refers to randomized algorithms that sample a small fraction of their inputs
Other settings where algorithms can run in sublinear time include
An algorithm is said to take linear time

Comparison sorts require at least

An algorithm is said to be of polynomial time if its running time is upper bounded by a polynomial expression in the size of the input

P : The complexity class of decision problems that can be solved on a deterministic Turing machine in polynomial time
NP : The complexity class of decision problems that can be solved on a non-deterministic Turing machine in polynomial time
ZPP : The complexity class of decision problems that can be solved with zero error on a probabilistic Turing machine in polynomial time
RP : The complexity class of decision problems that can be solved with 1-sided error on a probabilistic Turing machine in polynomial time.
BPP : The complexity class of decision problems that can be solved with 2-sided error on a probabilistic Turing machine in polynomial time
BQP : The complexity class of decision problems that can be solved with 2-sided error on a quantum Turing machine in polynomial time
P is the smallest time-complexity class on a deterministic machine which is robust in terms of machine model changes
An algorithm is defined to take superpolynomial time if T ( n ) is not bounded above by any polynomial
an algorithm that runs for 2 n steps on an input of size n requires superpolynomial time
the Adleman–Pomerance–Rumely primality test runs for n O (log log n ) time on n -bit inputs
Quasi-polynomial time algorithms are algorithms whose running time exhibits quasi-polynomial growth
a famous example is the directed Steiner tree problem
the planted clique problem in which the goal is to find a large clique in the union of a clique and a random graph
The complexity class QP consists of all problems that have quasi-polynomial time algorithms.
it is conjectured for many natural NP-complete problems that they do not have sub-exponential time algorithms
A problem is said to be sub-exponential time solvable if it can be solved in running times whose logarithms grow smaller than any given polynomial.
Some authors define sub-exponential time as running times in
the best-known classical algorithm for integer factorization, the general number field sieve
SUBEPT is the class of all parameterized problems that run in time sub-exponential in k and polynomial in the input size n
The exponential time hypothesis ( ETH ) is that 3SAT , the satisfiability problem of Boolean formulas in conjunctive normal form
The exponential time hypothesis implies P ≠ NP .
An algorithm is said to be exponential time , if T ( n ) is upper bounded by 2 poly( n )
Sometimes, exponential time is used to refer to algorithms that have T ( n ) = 2 O ( n )
An algorithm is said to be factorial time if T(n) is upper bounded by the factorial function n! .
An example of an algorithm that runs in factorial time is bogosort
An algorithm is said to be double exponential time if T ( n ) is upper bounded by 2 2 poly( n )
